thinking_summary:
**Decided:** Fix checkout timeout errors causing 3% cart abandonment. Metric: p95 checkout latency under 2s.
**Reasoning:** Support tickets spiked 40% in March, all pointing to payment gateway timeouts during peak hours. Current p95 is 8s.
**Considered and rejected:** Client-side retry (masks the problem), queue-based checkout (too complex for v1).
**Risks accepted:** Gateway provider SLA is 99.9% — residual timeout risk remains.

**Decided:** Timeout at 3s with graceful degradation — show processing state, retry once server-side, surface clear error after second failure.
**Reasoning:** Users tolerate 3s wait if progress is visible. Silent failures cause repeat submissions and double charges.
**Assumptions challenged:** Users will wait — added progress indicator requirement. One retry is enough — yes, data shows 95% of transient failures resolve on first retry.
**Risks accepted:** Double-charge window between retry and idempotency key propagation.

**Decided:** Wrap payment gateway calls in a circuit breaker with server-side retry. Add idempotency keys to prevent double charges.
**Reasoning:** Circuit breaker prevents cascade failures during gateway outages. Idempotency keys are the only reliable way to prevent duplicate charges across retries.
**Alternatives rejected:** Client-side polling (adds complexity, does not solve server timeout). Replace gateway (6-month migration, overkill).
**Codebase findings:** PaymentService already has a retry method but no idempotency — will extend existing interface.

**Decided:** Three files changed — PaymentService gets circuit breaker wrapper, CheckoutController gets timeout/retry logic, new IdempotencyStore backed by Redis.
**Reasoning:** Isolating circuit breaker in PaymentService keeps retry logic out of the controller. Redis-backed idempotency store handles multi-instance deployments.
**Structural issues found:** CheckoutController is 400 lines — extracting timeout handling into a middleware would be cleaner but is a separate refactor.
**Risks accepted:** Redis dependency for idempotency store adds infrastructure requirement. Accepted because Redis is already in the stack for sessions.

**Decided:** Let users control which notifications they receive and through which channels (email, push, in-app).
**Reasoning:** 12% of users disable all notifications because they cannot selectively mute. Granular preferences should recover half of those users.
**Considered and rejected:** AI-driven notification filtering (too opaque, users want explicit control). Per-message mute (too granular, UX nightmare).
**Risks accepted:** Preference migration for existing users — will default to current behavior, not opt everyone out.

**Decided:** Settings page with category toggles (marketing, transactional, social) and per-channel controls. Changes take effect immediately, no save button.
**Reasoning:** Category-level grouping keeps the UI manageable. Immediate effect matches user mental model — if I turn it off, it is off.
**Assumptions challenged:** Users understand notification categories — added tooltip descriptions for each category. Immediate save is safe — yes, preferences are non-destructive and reversible.
**Risks accepted:** Partial run — stopped after behavioral spec to discuss scope with team before proceeding to design.
